records.ety: [OE. gaderen, AS. gaderian, gadrian, fr. gador, geador, together, fr. gæd fellowship; akin to E. good, D. gaderen to collect, G. gatte husband, MHG. gate, also companion, Goth. gadiliggs a sister's son. √29. See Good, and cf. Together.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Gathered (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Gathering.]

defs: To bring together; to collect, as a number of separate things, into one place, or into one aggregate body; to assemble; to muster; to congregate.
quotes: And Belgium's capital had gathered them Her beauty and her chivalry.
When he had gathered all the chief priests and scribes of the people together.

defs: To pick out and bring together from among what is of less value; to collect, as a harvest; to harvest; to cull; to pick off; to pluck.
quotes: A rose just gathered from the stalk.
Do men gather grapes of thorns, or figs of thistles?
Gather us from among the heathen.

defs: To accumulate by collecting and saving little by little; to amass; to gain; to heap up.
quotes: He that by usury and unjust gain increaseth his substance, he shall gather it for him that will pity the poor.
To pay the creditor . . . he must gather up money by degrees.

defs: To bring closely together the parts or particles of; to contract; to compress; to bring together in folds or plaits, as a garment; also, to draw together, as a piece of cloth by a thread; to pucker; to plait; as, to gather a ruffle.
quotes: Gathering his flowing robe, he seemed to stand In act to speak, and graceful stretched his hand.

defs: To derive, or deduce, as an inference; to collect, as a conclusion, from circumstances that suggest, or arguments that prove; to infer; to conclude.
quotes: Let me say no more! Gather the sequel by that went before.
He gathers ground upon her in the chase.

defs: To bring together, or nearer together, in masonry, as where the width of a fireplace is rapidly diminished to the width of the flue, or the like.
To haul in; to take up; as, to gather the slack of a rope.
To come together; to collect; to unite; to become assembled; to congregate.
quotes: When small humors gather to a gout.
Tears from the depth of some divine despair Rise in the heart, and gather to the eyes.

defs: To grow larger by accretion; to increase.
quotes: Their snowball did not gather as it went.

defs: To concentrate; to come to a head, as a sore, and generate pus; as, a boil has gathered.
To collect or bring things together.
quotes: Thou knewest that I reap where I sowed not, and gather where I have not strewed.

defs: A plait or fold in cloth, made by drawing a thread through it; a pucker.
The inclination forward of the axle journals to keep the wheels from working outward.
The soffit or under surface of the masonry required in gathering. See Gather, v. t., 7.
